title: The Mansion Family

jokes.text: Kent's desperate attempts to share spotlight: 'And I'm Kent Brockman!' [lone cough] 'With Britney Spears!' [loud applause]
jokes.setup: Kent is co-hosting with teen sensation Britney Spears
jokes.punchline: His desperate re-announcements get progressively less applause, culminating in a lone cough
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalating structure showing Kent's desperation and ego. The lone cough is a brilliant audio punchline.

jokes.text: Homer: 'This is my year, Marge. Everyone knows I'm what makes this city great.' Marge: 'I don't know. There's a lot of buzz around Lenny.'
jokes.setup: Homer's confident declaration about being what makes Springfield great
jokes.punchline: Marge's matter-of-fact deflation suggesting Lenny is the frontrunner
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer/Marge dynamic - his delusion met with her practical deflation. Lenny as competition is absurd.

jokes.text: Krusty wins award, turns to person next to him: 'Who are you?' 'Seat filler.' 'Aw!'
jokes.setup: Krusty acknowledges someone sitting next to him at awards show
jokes.punchline: Person is literally just hired to fill empty seats, deflating any moment of connection
jokes.explanation: Efficient joke about the artifice of award shows. Krusty's disappointed 'Aw!' sells it.

jokes.text: Homer: 'Everyone gets an award but me. I can't help it if I donated the most blood. Ohh. I'm feeling kinda woozy.'
jokes.setup: Homer complains about not getting recognition
jokes.punchline: Reveals he donated excessive blood (presumably for money) and is now suffering effects
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - complaining while simultaneously revealing his own poor judgment. The physical consequence pays it off.

jokes.text: Marge: 'You won a Grammy.' Homer: 'I mean an award that's worth winning!'
jokes.setup: Marge tries to console Homer by reminding him of his Grammy win
jokes.punchline: Homer dismisses a Grammy as not worth winning
jokes.explanation: Solid callback joke that shows Homer's selective memory and impossible standards.

jokes.text: Britney: 'in today's youth-obsessed culture we sometimes forget that older people are still alive.' Kent: 'I told ya!' Britney: 'Well, sorry!'
jokes.setup: Britney makes observation about ageism in culture
jokes.punchline: Kent takes it personally and Britney awkwardly apologizes
jokes.explanation: Nice awkward moment playing on Kent's age insecurity and the artifice of celebrity hosting.

jokes.text: I told ya! Well, sorry!
jokes.setup: Britney makes a comment about older people still being alive
jokes.punchline: The awkward realization she just insulted their award recipient
jokes.explanation: Simple awkwardness moment - functional but not particularly clever

jokes.text: Britney: 'In the market crash of 1929 he helped people jump out of windows to avoid disgrace.' Cornelius: 'You're doing the right thing. Oh, oh! Out you go! My, you're a big one. No, no turning back now. Off a tall building- That's a great way to do it!'
jokes.setup: Describing Cornelius helping during the stock market crash
jokes.punchline: He enthusiastically encouraged people to commit suicide, treating it like customer service
jokes.explanation: Exceptionally dark humor treating suicide assistance as cheerful customer service. The escalating enthusiasm is horrifying and hilarious.

jokes.text: You're doing the right thing. Oh, oh! Out you go! My, you're a big one. No, no turning back now. Off a tall building- That's a great way to do it!
jokes.setup: The previous joke about helping people jump
jokes.punchline: Enthusiastic coaching dialogue for suicide assistance
jokes.explanation: Escalates the dark humor with cheerful coaching - the enthusiasm makes it even more twisted

jokes.text: Cornelius dies during his introduction, Kent: 'Well, guess we should've expected that.'
jokes.setup: 108-year-old man dies while receiving award
jokes.punchline: Kent's matter-of-fact acknowledgment that this was predictable
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan reaction to an absurd but predictable situation. Kent's professional composure makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Homer sits down when asked for 100-year-olds after standing for everyone 60+
jokes.setup: Looking for progressively older people to give award to
jokes.punchline: Homer stood up for 60+ but sits when they get specific about 100+
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer opportunism - willing to lie about age until the lie becomes too big

jokes.text: Homer's acceptance: 'Don't give me that kiss of death, you black widow! Boy, he didn't want to let go of that!'
jokes.setup: Homer receiving award after previous winner died
jokes.punchline: Calls Britney a 'black widow' and comments on the dead man's grip
jokes.explanation: Homer's inappropriate but logical reaction to the situation. 'Black widow' is a great callback to the previous death.

jokes.text: abra-cadaver!
jokes.setup: Homer's impromptu acceptance speech
jokes.punchline: Pun combining 'abracadabra' with 'cadaver' after someone just died
jokes.explanation: Clever dark pun that's perfectly inappropriate for the moment

jokes.text: Homer's speech: 'abra-cadaver!'
jokes.setup: Homer giving impromptu acceptance speech
jokes.punchline: Combines 'abracadabra' with 'cadaver' given the death context
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that's both stupid and clever. Perfect Homer malapropism that actually works in context.

jokes.text: Homer reads prepared speech about loving Springfield 'from cuddliest infant to the—' then improvises: 'Puppies. Patriotism. Bluebirds. [raspberry] I'm not reading this drivel.'
jokes.setup: Homer starts reading generic feel-good speech
jokes.punchline: Abandons it mid-sentence, calls it drivel, and makes raspberry sound
jokes.explanation: Meta-commentary on generic award speeches. Homer's complete lack of filter and the raspberry punctuation is perfect.

jokes.text: Marge: 'That's not an award. That's part of the set.' Homer: 'Nothing you can say will diminish this honor.'
jokes.setup: Marge tries to reality-check Homer about his 'award'
jokes.punchline: Homer refuses to let facts interfere with his moment of glory
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer delusion. His determination to maintain the fantasy despite obvious facts is very in character.

jokes.text: Burns: 'Who's that fellow who always screws up and creates havoc?' Smithers: 'Homer Simpson, sir?' Burns: 'Yes! The way I figure it, he's due for a good performance.'
jokes.setup: Burns needs someone to house-sit and describes someone who creates havoc
jokes.punchline: Chooses Homer based on gambler's fallacy logic
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns logic - acknowledging Homer's track record while using faulty reasoning to justify choosing him anyway.

jokes.text: Who's that fellow who always screws up and creates havoc? Homer Simpson, sir? Yes! The way I figure it, he's due for a good performance.
jokes.setup: Burns needs someone to watch his house
jokes.punchline: Burns chooses the worst possible person because he thinks law of averages will help
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy - Burns' twisted logic perfectly sets up the episode's premise

jokes.text: Burns: 'if the house catches fire I want you to call this number.' Homer: 'The fire department.' Burns: 'Yes. They're new, but they're good.'
jokes.setup: Burns giving Homer emergency instructions
jokes.punchline: Burns describes the fire department as 'new' organization
jokes.explanation: Burns' extreme old age shown through treating fire departments as newfangled innovation. Perfect character consistency.

jokes.text: Homer: 'Look at me! I'm a billionaire!' [Falls down stairs]
jokes.setup: Homer celebrating his temporary wealth status
jokes.punchline: Immediately falls down the mansion stairs
jokes.explanation: Basic physical comedy that undercuts Homer's moment of triumph. Simple but effective.

jokes.text: Marge: 'And the bed never needs to be made. Check it out.' [Makes bed automatically]
jokes.setup: Marge discovers self-making bed feature
jokes.punchline: The visual of an automatic bed-making system
jokes.explanation: Visual gag about absurd luxury. Marge's practical appreciation of household labor-saving device is in character.

jokes.text: Homer demonstrates automatic dresser: gets violently dressed by machine, emerges disheveled: 'Now I'm ready to hit the town.'
jokes.setup: Homer tries automatic dressing machine
jokes.punchline: Machine violently dresses him, result looks terrible but he's satisfied
jokes.explanation: Good visual slapstick with Homer's oblivious satisfaction as the punchline. Mechanical automation gone wrong.

jokes.text: Lisa finds Burns' Nancy Drew collection: 'even the controversial Clue in the Clock. Tsk-tsk-tsk. So many swears.'
jokes.setup: Lisa discovers Burns has complete Nancy Drew collection
jokes.punchline: Nancy Drew book is 'controversial' and full of swearing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor - Nancy Drew books as scandalous literature. Perfect inversion of expectations about children's mystery books.

jokes.text: Bart: 'I'm Al Unser Jr.!' Lisa: 'I'm Princess Margaret!' Bart: 'I'm drunk!'
jokes.setup: Kids playing dress-up with fancy costumes, naming famous people
jokes.punchline: Bart escalates from race car driver to being intoxicated
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from celebrity impersonation to drunkenness. Bart's inappropriate behavior is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Burns filling out medical forms: Social Security number: 'Naught-naught-naught, naught-naught, naught-naught-naught-two.'
jokes.setup: Burns filling out standard medical paperwork
jokes.punchline: His SSN is essentially 000-00-0002, indicating he was among the first issued
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke establishing Burns' extreme age through bureaucratic detail. The 'naught' pronunciation adds old-fashioned charm.

jokes.text: Burns: 'Damn Roosevelt! Cause of parents' death: Got in my way.'
jokes.setup: Burns continues filling out medical history
jokes.punchline: Blames FDR, then casually states he killed his own parents for inconveniencing him
jokes.explanation: Peak Burns evil casualness. The transition from political complaint to patricide confession is masterfully done.

jokes.text: Marge: 'This all seems a little elaborate for sloppy joes.' Homer: 'I know what the other 11 forks are for, but what do you do with this one?' Lisa: 'Why, Marge, I believe you're supposed to scratch your ass with it.'
jokes.setup: Family confused by elaborate dining setup for simple meal
jokes.punchline: Lisa delivers crude answer in sophisticated British accent
jokes.explanation: Perfect contrast between Lisa's affected sophistication and crude content. The British accent makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Marge: 'Cigars are for rich people and legendary comedians like Bill Crosby and David Letterson.'
jokes.setup: Marge trying to stop Homer from smoking expensive cigars
jokes.punchline: Mispronounces Bill Cosby as 'Crosby' and David Letterman as 'Letterson'
jokes.explanation: Marge's innocent mispronunciations of celebrities. Not groundbreaking but fits her character's occasional malapropisms.

jokes.text: Homer: 'Jimmy is such an ugly word, Marge, unless you're talking about Jimmy Smits.' [lascivious purr]
jokes.setup: Marge accuses Homer of jimmying open Burns' liquor cabinet
jokes.punchline: Homer finds exception for actor Jimmy Smits and purrs suggestively
jokes.explanation: Random Homer attraction to specific actor. The purr sells the absurdity of his sudden arousal.

jokes.text: Homer calling international numbers: 'Operator, get me Thailand. T-I- and so on.' Later: 'How's everything on your end? That's some language you got there. And you talk like that 24-7, huh?'
jokes.setup: Homer making expensive international calls on Burns' phone
jokes.punchline: His incredibly casual conversation with Thailand about their language
jokes.explanation: Homer's oblivious cultural insensitivity presented as friendly curiosity. The 24-7 question is particularly absurd.

jokes.text: Homer: 'We do have to have a party!' Marge: 'No parties!' Homer: 'What about par-tay?' Marge: 'No par-tays, no shindigs, no keggers, no hootenannies, no mixers, no raves, no box socials.'
jokes.setup: Homer wants to throw party, Marge objects
jokes.punchline: Marge's comprehensive list of prohibited party types across multiple eras
jokes.explanation: Good escalating list joke with variety of party terminology spanning different time periods. Marge's thoroughness is funny.

jokes.text: Doctor examining Burns: 'Well, isn't that odd? It's like poking through meringue.' Burns: 'Oh, try this arm. I saw some blood in there the other day.'
jokes.setup: Doctor trying to draw blood from extremely old Burns
jokes.punchline: Burns' matter-of-fact suggestion about which arm might have blood
jokes.explanation: Excellent medical horror comedy. Burns' casual attitude toward his decomposing body is both disturbing and hilarious.

jokes.text: Moe: 'How about Tuborg, the beer of Danish kings?' Homer: 'Mmm. Danish.'
jokes.setup: Moe offers Danish beer with pretentious description
jokes.punchline: Homer's Pavlovian response thinking of Danish pastries instead
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer food obsession overriding everything else. The 'Mmm' reaction is signature Homer behavior.

jokes.text: Lenny and Carl: 'We're just watching the sun move across the sky. When it gets to here, we can drink again.'
jokes.setup: Homer asks why they're at bar if no beer sales until 2pm
jokes.punchline: They're literally watching sun position like a primitive sundial to track drinking time
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly pathetic dedication to drinking. The primitive sun-watching method elevates simple alcoholism to cosmic patience.

jokes.text: Moe: 'Once you get 12 miles out, there's no laws at all. That's where they held the Tyson/Secretariat fight.' Homer: 'They were so drunk!'
jokes.setup: Moe explains international waters have no laws
jokes.punchline: References impossible boxing match between Mike Tyson and racehorse Secretariat, Homer focuses on drinking
jokes.explanation: Absolutely brilliant absurdist premise - human vs horse boxing match. Homer's focus on the drinking aspect rather than impossibility is perfect.

jokes.text: Homer steering boat: 'Turn! Left! Boat go there!' with passengers cheering chaotically
jokes.setup: Homer attempting to captain Burns' yacht
jokes.punchline: His primitive steering commands and the boat's erratic movement
jokes.explanation: Homer's incompetence at sea. The caveman-like steering commands show his complete lack of nautical knowledge.

jokes.text: Man on ship: 'They're rebroadcasting major league baseball with implied oral consent, not express written consent, or so the legend goes.'
jokes.setup: Explaining what illegal activities happen in international waters
jokes.punchline: MLB's copyright warning becomes legendary piracy in international waters
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of MLB's copyright paranoia. Making it legendary maritime piracy elevates mundane legal language to epic status.

jokes.text: They're rebroadcasting major league baseball with implied oral consent... not express written consent, or so the legend goes.
jokes.setup: Showing what's possible in lawless international waters
jokes.punchline: References the specific legal language from MLB broadcasts
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta joke about copyright language that sports fans would immediately recognize

jokes.text: Sea Captain: 'Arr, I now pronounce ya man and cow.' [Cow moos]
jokes.setup: International waters allowing anything illegal
jokes.punchline: Wedding ceremony between human and livestock
jokes.explanation: Absurd extension of 'no laws' concept. The cow's moo as response to wedding vows adds to the surrealism.

jokes.text: Doctor: 'Mr. Burns, I'm afraid you are the sickest man in the United States. You have everything.' Burns: 'You mean, I have pneumonia?' 'Yes.' 'Juvenile diabetes?' 'Yes.' 'Hysterical pregnancy?' 'Uh, a little bit, yes.'
jokes.setup: Doctor explains Burns has every disease
jokes.punchline: Escalating through various diseases including impossible ones for his gender/age
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating medical absurdity. 'Hysterical pregnancy' in an ancient man is particularly brilliant, doctor's hesitant confirmation perfect.

jokes.text: Mr. Burns, I'm afraid you are the sickest man in the United States. You have everything.
jokes.setup: Doctor delivering medical results to Burns
jokes.punchline: Having 'everything' medically means having every disease
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay that subverts expectations about 'having everything' in a medical context

jokes.text: Doctor's explanation with visual aids: 'Here is the door to your body. You see? And these are oversized novelty germs. That's influenza, that's bronchitis, and this cute little cuddle-bug is pancreatic cancer.'
jokes.setup: Doctor explaining Burns' medical condition with props
jokes.punchline: Referring to pancreatic cancer as 'cute little cuddle-bug'
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy masterpiece - making terminal cancer adorable. The contrast between cutesy language and deadly disease is perfect.

jokes.text: this cute little cuddle-bug is pancreatic cancer
jokes.setup: Doctor explaining Burns' diseases with cute names
jokes.punchline: Refers to deadly cancer with adorable terminology
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor contrasting cute language with deadly disease

jokes.text: Doctor demonstrates Three Stooges syndrome with germs: 'Move it, chowder head!' Burns: 'So what you're saying is I'm indestructible.' Doctor: 'Oh, no, no. In fact, even a slight breeze could—' Burns: 'Indestructible.'
jokes.setup: Doctor explains how all diseases cancel each other out
jokes.punchline: Burns ignores doctor's warning about fragility and declares himself indestructible
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns character moment - selective hearing for good news only. 'Indestructible' became an iconic Burns quote.

jokes.text: So what you're saying is I'm indestructible. Oh, no, no. In fact, even a slight breeze could- Indestructible.
jokes.setup: Doctor explains Burns' precarious health balance
jokes.punchline: Burns ignores the warning and focuses only on the positive interpretation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns character comedy - his selective hearing and twisted optimism shine through

jokes.text: Homer looking at land people: 'Look at those poor saps back on land with their laws and ethics. They'll never know the simple joys of a monkey knife fight.'
jokes.setup: Homer feeling superior about being in international waters
jokes.punchline: Considers monkey knife fighting a 'simple joy' denied to law-abiding citizens
jokes.explanation: Absolutely brilliant line. 'Simple joys of a monkey knife fight' is peak Simpsons absurdism - treating horrific spectacle as wholesome entertainment.

jokes.text: Coast Guard: 'Looks like another homosexual party boat. They always have such nice things. Perhaps we should pay them a visit. A deadly visit? Well, let's play it by ear.'
jokes.setup: Coast Guard spotting Homer's party boat
jokes.punchline: Casual discussion of potentially deadly raid based on stereotypes and envy
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about Coast Guard corruption and prejudice. The casual 'play it by ear' approach to violence is darkly funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Pirates board boat. Homer: 'Are you friendly pirates?' Pirate: 'Uh, not really, no.'
jokes.setup: Pirates attacking the party boat
jokes.punchline: Homer's naive question gets honest, matter-of-fact answer
jokes.explanation: Homer's innocent optimism meeting brutal reality. Pirate's polite honesty about unfriendliness is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Lenny getting robbed: 'Whoa! They're poking every nook and cranny- well, every cranny anyway. So far, the nook is relatively— Oh, no! No, it isn't!'
jokes.setup: Lenny describing being searched by pirates
jokes.punchline: His attempts to find silver lining about his 'nook' being safe, immediately contradicted
jokes.explanation: Good escalating joke with Lenny's optimism being systematically destroyed. The nook/cranny wordplay adds cleverness.

jokes.text: Homer fighting pirates, gets rope burn: 'Oh! Oh! Charley horse! Charley horse! My mouth! I, eh— In the net, right?'
jokes.setup: Homer attempting heroic fight against pirates
jokes.punchline: Immediately injured by minor hazards, gives up and accepts capture
jokes.explanation: Homer's predictable failure at heroics. The immediate surrender after minor injuries is very in character.

jokes.text: Carl: 'Oh, we're gonna die, and I never tasted cantaloupe!' Homer: 'Eh, you didn't miss much. Honeydew is the money melon.'
jokes.setup: Carl's deathbed regret about never trying cantaloupe
jokes.punchline: Homer dismisses it and promotes honeydew as 'money melon'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer food wisdom in crisis. 'Money melon' is brilliant slang that sounds authoritative while being nonsensical.

jokes.text: Pirate: 'And now we will cut you loose. For liability purposes, it is the ocean that will kill you, not us.'
jokes.setup: Pirates about to kill captives
jokes.punchline: Modern liability concerns applied to traditional piracy
jokes.explanation: Excellent satire of modern legal disclaimers applied to murder. The bureaucratization of piracy is brilliantly absurd.

jokes.text: For liability purposes, it is the ocean that will kill you, not us.
jokes.setup: Pirates about to throw everyone overboard
jokes.punchline: Pirates are concerned about legal liability even while committing piracy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor about modern legal concerns in classic piracy context

jokes.text: Homer on floating in net: 'What about the people on the bottom?' 'They're the greatest heroes of all.' Guy at bottom: 'Hey! Something's clawing at my leg! Okay, it stopped.'
jokes.setup: Homer asks about people at bottom of floating net
jokes.punchline: Homer's noble rhetoric immediately followed by bottom person's sea creature attack
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about survival hierarchy. Homer's empty heroic rhetoric contrasted with actual suffering below.

jokes.text: Burns: 'Echh! The foul stench of youth!'
jokes.setup: Burns returning home to find evidence of young people
jokes.punchline: His disgusted reaction to youth as literally malodorous
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns misanthropy. Treating youth as literally offensive smell rather than metaphorically is very in character.

jokes.text: Burns discovers damaged monkey: 'Furious George! What have they done to your beautiful face?' Smithers: 'This monkey is going to need most of your skin.'
jokes.setup: Burns discovers his fighting monkey has been injured
jokes.punchline: Smithers matter-of-factly states monkey will need Burns' skin for grafting
jokes.explanation: Good callback to monkey knife fight with dark medical consequence. Smithers' casual delivery of horrific news is perfect.

jokes.text: Homer looking at Forbes list, sobbing: 'I can't even finish! I want to be rich! Like these guys!' Continues crying: 'Look at all these rich people here! Not as rich as they should be, of course, but still rich!'
jokes.setup: Homer reading Forbes richest people list
jokes.punchline: Even while coveting wealth, he criticizes the rich for not being rich enough
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - even his envy is contradictory. Criticizing billionaires for insufficient wealth while wanting their money shows his confused values.

tmdb_overview: Worried about his health, Mr. Burns visits the Mayo Clinic. He lets Homer housesit his mansion, which leads of course to an offshore knife fight and an attack by Chinese pirates.
